content.heading: Before we start coding — think about this:

blocks.text: You're building a game and you want to greet the player by name. But you don't know who's going to play it. You can't write `let name = 'Alex'` because it won't always be Alex.

items: Could the code figure it out on its own?
Could we tell the program to ask the user for their name?

content.heading: We can make the code interactive!

blocks.text: So far your programs only work with values you wrote yourself. That's not very useful. Real programs respond to the person using them. `prompt()` pauses your program, shows a pop-up box, and waits for the user to type something. Whatever they type comes back to you as a string.

blocks.code: let name = prompt('What is your name?')
blocks.desc: Show a pop-up asking 'What is your name?' and store whatever the user types into 'name'

content.heading: What happens if you are prompting for a number?

blocks.text: prompt() always gives you back a string, with no exceptions. Even if the user types `42`, you get the text `'42'`, not the number `42`. That means you can't do math with it yet. `'42' + 1` gives you `'421'`, not `43`. The fix: wrap `prompt()` in `Number()` and it converts the answer the moment the user hits OK.

blocks.code: let age = prompt('How old are you?')
blocks.desc: Returns the string '13' even if the user typed 13. Looks like a number, but it's text, so math won't work.

blocks.code: let age = Number(prompt('How old are you?'))
blocks.desc: Number() converts the string '13' into the actual number 13. Now you can add, subtract, and multiply normally.

blocks.alt: Visual showing prompt() returning a string vs Number(prompt()) returning a number

blocks.label: Always convert if you're doing math
blocks.text: prompt() returns a string every single time , even if the user types a number. '13' is not the same as 13. If you skip Number(), you'll get weird results like '13' + 1 = '131' instead of 14.

exercises.title: What does prompt() do?
tasks: What happens when the browser encounters the prompt() function?
exercises.hint: Think about what happens to the execution flow and the user's screen.

options.label: It quietly prints text to the background console.
It pauses the program and opens a pop-up box for user text entry.
It automatically generates a random name for the player.
It permanently stops the script and crashes the game.

exercises.explanation: The prompt() function completely pauses the program execution, presents an interactive pop-up box to the user, and waits for them to type an answer and click OK.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Predicting prompt() data types
tasks: If a player inputs the digits 55 into a standard prompt() box, what type is returned?
exercises.hint: Review the core rule regarding prompt() outputs.
exercises.explanation: prompt() always returns a string, with absolutely no exceptions.[cite: 3] Even if the user only types numbers, it arrives inside quotes as text like '55'.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Categorizing inputs and conversions
tasks: Classify these expressions into the data type they evaluate to after execution.
exercises.hint: Check if Number() wraps the expression or if quotes are left on.
exercises.explanation: Any naked prompt() call produces a string.[cite: 3] Wrapping that prompt() call inside a Number() function forces the value into a numerical data type.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Drop the interaction tokens
tasks: Complete the script to show a prompt window and assign the value to the username variable.
exercises.hint: Use the standard assignment symbol and the function keyword that gathers text input.
codeWithBlanks: let userName ___BLANK___ ___BLANK___('Who is playing Simon Says?');
exercises.explanation: The `=` symbol sets up assignment, and `prompt` kicks off the interactive popup dialogue window to fetch information.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Avoid string concatenation traps
tasks: Drop tokens into place so that the custom rounds value undergoes proper type conversion.
exercises.hint: You need to wrap the whole prompt structure with the standard numerical converter block.
exercises.explanation: Nesting `prompt()` within `Number()` instantly transforms the incoming input text line into a live, usable math number.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Interactive flow timeline
tasks: Arrange the code lines so that input is requested, processed, and displayed in sequence.
exercises.hint: You must capture the incoming text in a container before references to it can exist.
exercises.explanation: First, prompt data collection outputs a raw string value.[cite: 3] Next, it gets converted to a true number so math calculation operators work properly instead of glomming text segments together.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Construct the input collection
tasks: Type out the missing system commands to ask the user for their home region choice.
exercises.hint: Write the exact lowercase keyword utilized for calling interactive dialog inputs.
codeWithBlanks: let playerRegion = ___BLANK___('Which region are you in?');
exercises.explanation: The built-in function `prompt` creates a text box dynamically on runtime execution.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Type evaluation tracing
tasks: What outputs to the system console when evaluating this exact block?
exercises.hint: Trace out type propagation rules for standard + syntax operators when strings mix with real numbers.
exercises.explanation: Because rawInput stores the string '3', adding 1 tells JavaScript to perform string concatenation, joining '3' and 1 into '31'.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Input parsing assembly
tasks: Supply both necessary functional wrappers to collect input text and instantly cast it to a numerical format.
exercises.hint: The parsing block wraps entirely around the dialog display block.
exercises.explanation: Nesting `prompt()` inside `Number()` executes input evaluation safely so calculations run reliably.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Your first interactive program
tasks: Use prompt() to ask the user for their name.
Store it in a variable called userName.
Use a template literal to log: 'Nice to meet you, [name]! Welcome to Curia.'
Run it and type your name in the pop-up.
exercises.hint: The string inside prompt() is the message the user sees.[cite: 3] Whatever they type becomes your variable's value.[cite: 3]

steps.text: Complete the program sequence by logging the template literal string greeting format matching instructions.

exercises.explanation: Capturing names dynamically removes the limitation of static hardcoded string templates inside game greeting logic architectures.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Age calculator
tasks: Ask the user how old they are using prompt().
Convert the input to a number with Number().
Calculate how old they'll be in 5 years.
Log the result to the console matching the requested structure.
exercises.hint: Number(prompt('...')) handles input delivery and dynamic mathematical extraction perfectly in one line.[cite: 3]

steps.text: Add a console log command that calculates and outputs age + 5 values clearly inside backticks text patterns.

exercises.explanation: If math operators ran across non-converted strings directly from input interfaces, evaluation errors accumulate fast.[cite: 3]

exercises.title: Build a personal fact card
tasks: Ask the user for their target rounds quota using prompt.
Convert it directly using standard explicit Number methods.
Log out the value multiplied by 2 so double matching expectations verify.
exercises.hint: Wrap the selection input step entirely inside your targeted casting call expressions.[cite: 3]

steps.text: Initialize the variable called luckyNumber by combining Number and prompt targets.

exercises.explanation: Casting values cleanly from prompt structures handles smooth state handling pipeline transitions across game logic execution blocks.[cite: 3]

challenges.title: The personalised greeting
challenges.description: A game wants to welcome the player by name and tell them which level they're starting on. Right now it just prints a generic message. Fix the code so it asks for the player's name using prompt(), then prints a personalised welcome using a template literal. Then extend it to ask what level they want to start on and include that too.
challenges.hint: Replace the hardcoded values with prompt() calls. The level prompt returns a string, so you don't need Number() here since you're just displaying it, not doing math.[cite: 3]

checks.label: message includes the level in the right format

challenges.explanation: prompt() lets the user provide their own value at runtime instead of you hardcoding it.[cite: 3] Replace let name = 'Player' with let name = prompt('What is your name?') and let level = 1 with let level = prompt('What level do you want to start on?').[cite: 3] The template literal then drops both values in automatically.[cite: 3]

challenges.title: The tip calculator
challenges.description: A restaurant bill is £45. The code asks the user for a tip percentage using prompt() and tries to calculate the tip, but the numbers come out wrong. Diagnose the type issue using typeof, fix it with Number(), then log: 'Tip: £[amount]. Total: £[total].'
challenges.hint: typeof tipPercent will reveal the problem. Wrap tipPercent in Number() before doing the math. Notice how * works differently than + when mixing strings and numbers.[cite: 3]
challenges.explanation: prompt() always returns a string.[cite: 3] When you use * with a string that looks like a number, JavaScript actually converts it silently, so the multiplication might work.[cite: 3] But + is the dangerous one: bill + tip might join instead of add if tip is still a string.[cite: 3] Always wrap prompt() in Number() when doing math: let tipPercent = Number(prompt(...)).[cite: 3]

challenges.title: The Simon Says intro sequence
challenges.description: Build a mini intro sequence for the Simon Says game. Ask the player for their name, age, and how many rounds they want to play. Then log a single summary message and check whether they qualify as a junior player.
challenges.hint: The summary will work fine without Number(). But the age comparison < 10 won't work correctly when playerAge is a string. Use typeof to confirm, then wrap it in Number() just for the comparison.[cite: 3]

checks.label: logs the player tier status text cleanly

challenges.explanation: prompt() returns strings for all values.[cite: 3] The summary template literal works fine with strings.[cite: 3] But playerAge < 10 compares a string to a number, and JavaScript tries to convert it, and it mostly works, but it's unreliable and considered a bug.[cite: 3] The fix: wrap only playerAge in Number() when comparing: if (Number(playerAge) < 10).[cite: 3]
